meta.title: Introduction & Greetings
meta.topic: Basic greetings, introductions, and the verb être

verbs.notes: Irregular verb — must memorise. Most important verb in French.
examples: Je suis étudiant. — I am a student.
Elle est professeur. — She is a teacher.

verbs.meaning: to be called / to call oneself
verbs.notes: Reflexive verb. Note double-L in je/tu/il/ils forms.
examples: Je m'appelle Marie. — My name is Marie.
Comment vous appelez-vous? — What is your name? (formal)

grammar.title: No subject omission
grammar.explanation: Unlike Spanish/Italian, French always requires the subject pronoun. You cannot say just 'suis étudiant' — you must say 'je suis étudiant'.

grammar.title: Tu vs Vous
grammar.explanation: Tu is singular informal (friends, family, children). Vous is singular formal (strangers, professionals) OR plural for any group. When in doubt, use vous.

grammar.title: Gender agreement
grammar.explanation: Adjectives agree with the noun they describe. Enchanté (masculine) → enchantée (feminine). You will see this pattern everywhere.

key_phrases.english: How are you? / How's it going?

cultural_notes.title: La bise
cultural_notes.content: French greeting involves cheek kisses (la bise) — typically 2 in Paris, varies by region (1–4). Between men this is less common; a handshake is standard. Don't be surprised when it happens!

cultural_notes.title: Formality matters
cultural_notes.content: Starting with 'vous' is always safe. Switching to 'tu' is called 'tutoyer' and usually happens when the other person suggests it or in casual contexts. Never assume 'tu' with older adults or in professional settings.

items.prompt: Nice to meet you. (said by a woman)
